value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-8873-SHNH-22RV-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been -0.1% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 36.7% or 9.5% annualized.

In comparison, Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.'s growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 2.3% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 49% or 11.6% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $143.50 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.2. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.66 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.'s last trading price was $79.73 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 18.9. With an EPS growth rate of 26.9 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 0.7 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

urn:contentItem:6JHM-DPX3-RY2J-53P0-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags CMS Energy Corporation2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags CMS Energy Corporation

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been -0.1% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 36.7% or 9.5% annualized.

In comparison, CMS Energy Corporation's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 5.9% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 33.9% or 9.1% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $143.50 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.2. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.66 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

CMS Energy Corporation's last trading price was $74.93 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 21.2. With an EPS growth rate of 7.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.87 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

value.Title: On Again, Off Again Solar Project Off
value.Overview: The Allegan County News, The & Union Enterprise (Michigan), NEWS; Pg. A5, 538words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ6-2T83-RY91-1557-00000-00On Again, Off Again Solar Project Off2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A5On Again, Off Again Solar Project OffJoseph Schultz

In the on-again/off-again back-and-forth between NorthStar Clean Energy's solar project and Monterey and Hopkins townships, it looks like it is "off" again.

Monterey Supervisor Henry Reinart told the township board that NorthStar sent a letter stating it is withdrawing its special use application and will instead seek approval from the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC).

Reinart said the reason NorthStar gave for going to the MPSC was Monterey's lack of a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) ordinance.

Instead of waiting for Monterey to craft one, NorthStar decided to appeal to the state.

Although NorthStar expressed its intent to appeal to the MPSC, it has not yet sent an official withdrawal to Lori Castello of Professional Code Inspections (PCI). Castello advised the township that until she receives official notification, the solar project should remain on the agenda for the planning meeting on May 11.

In January, NorthStar Clean Energy - Allegan Solar LLC told the Monterey Planning Commission that a 101-megawatt solar energy system and a 200-megawatt-hour BESS project would be built in Monterey and Hopkins townships.

NorthStar is a renewable energy company owned by CMS Energy Corporation, which also owns Consumers Energy.

In February, NorthStar sought a zoning variance from the Hopkins Zoning Board of Appeals (ZBA) to build closer than 300 feet from a non-associated dwelling. The company also requested to build closer than 100 feet from a 100-year floodplain and a wetland.

The request was not heard due to an error in the official notifications. In March, NorthStar withdrew the request. Senior Manager Colin Daining explained that the company was "looking at a different approach." At the March Monterey planning meeting, it was revealed that Monterey did not have a "compatible renewable energy ordinance" regarding the BESS. Township lawyers and engineers were scheduled to meet with NorthStar's counterparts to resolve the issues.

Last month, NorthStar failed to submit an expected application to Hopkins Township. Amid reports of an MPSC appeal, Township Supervisor Cade Bolser clarified that the letter the township received was NorthStar's request to meet with the township to talk about the township's solar ordinance, and its alignment with state requirements in Public Act 233.(PA 233) There has been no word on those discussions.

Reinart stated it was his understanding that the entire project is moving to the MPSC. However, that has not been verified with Hopkins Township.

According to the letter, NorthStar will file its appeal by the end of June. Under state law, Michigan will compensate the townships for the costs of presenting their defense to the MPSC.

In 2023, the Michigan Legislature passed PA 233 to help reach Governor Gretchen Whitmer's goal of 100% renewable energy production in the state. The act grants the MPSC the power to approve special use permits over the objections of local authorities.

This appeal process is becoming a popular avenue for renewable energy projects. Just last month, Silver Maple PV, LLC filed an appeal for a solar project in Jamestown and Zeeland townships. That case is still ongoing.

Editor's Note: This story courtesy of TownBroadcast

urn:contentItem:6JHM-DPW3-RXW9-W141-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Lags NiSource Inc.2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Lags NiSource Inc.

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 7.4% annualized over 10 years.

In comparison, NiSource Inc.'s growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 8.1% annualized over 10 years.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $142.44 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.1. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.63 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

NiSource Inc.'s last trading price was $47.51 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 24.2. With an EPS growth rate of 2.1 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 11.39 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 3-Year Earnings Growth Lags Nextera Energy, Inc.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH6-5683-S8J5-X4W3-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Lags Nextera Energy, Inc.2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Lags Nextera Energy, Inc.

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 8.4% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 41.5% or 14.9% annualized.

In comparison, Nextera Energy, Inc.'s growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 16.4% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 35.8% or 12.9% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $146.73 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 20.8. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.49 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Nextera Energy, Inc.'s last trading price was $95.54 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 28.9. With an EPS growth rate of 16.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 1.76 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

urn:contentItem:6JK3-WT63-RSWV-8466-00000-00DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform Exelon Corporation by 3.7% in the past month2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform Exelon Corporation by 3.7% in the past month

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's (NYSE:DTE) share price is continuing to outperform that of Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC). DTE Energy Company's return over the past month has been -5.1% and Exelon Corporation's return has been -8.9% over the same period.

Here is how DTE Energy Company and Exelon Corporation compare:

Company Name1 Month %3 Month%YTD %1 yr %DTE Energy Company-5.1-1.311.010.5Exelon Corporation-8.9-7.92.45.7

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Exelon Corporation operates as a leading regulated utility holding company providing electricity and natural gas to nearly 11 million residential, commercial, and industrial customers across its service territories. The core business consists of electricity transmission and distribution operations through established subsidiaries like ComEd, PECO, BGE, and PHI utilities. The largest revenue sources derive directly from electric delivery services and natural gas distribution, which benefit from approved distribution and transmission rates designed to enhance grid reliability. The fastest-growing segments include the distribution and transmission rate bases at ComEd, BGE, and PHI, driven by higher capital expenditures, favorable weather impacts, and targeted infrastructure investments. By balancing consistent capital deployment with cost management, the enterprise serves widespread end markets across multiple states with highly essential energy infrastructure services.

value.Title: McKinney puts forth community solar legislation centered on individuals hardest hit by energy costs

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK7-S7C3-RYY3-G0R2-00000-00McKinney puts forth community solar legislation centered on individuals hardest hit by energy costs2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisMcKinney puts forth community solar legislation centered on individuals hardest hit by energy costs

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Kyle Davidson

May 14th, 2026 ( Michigan Advance - Delivered by Newstex )

Rep. Donavan McKinney (D-Detroit) announces his plan to introduce community solar legislation focused on supporting low income households and organized labor. May 13, 2026 | Photo by Kyle Davidson/Michigan Advance

Improving access to renewable energy has been a perennial issue among Michigan lawmakers, one that has often cut across party lines.

From eliminating the cap on customers generating their own energy, to allowing residents to join community solar programs, lawmakers and clean energy advocates have repeatedly argued that rooftop and local access to renewable energy will bring both cost savings and reliability benefits to Michigan residents.

During a press conference on Wednesday, state Rep. Donavan McKinney (D-Detroit) offered another proposal to create a legal structure for community solar programs, where residents can subscribe to an array of solar panels - often built on a brownfield, rooftop, parking lot or unused land - and receive credit on their energy bill for the power it produces.

'For years, Michigan families have asked for more options to lower their energy bills and participate in the clean energy economy, but for too many people, renters, low income households, those with shaded roofs or aging homes, rooftop solar simply isn't possible,' McKinney said. 'Community solar is a solution that meets people where they are.'

While community solar policies have been introduced and reintroduced, McKinney said his legislation takes a more progressive approach that places consumers, environmentalists and labor unions first.

Rep. Donavan McKinney (D-Detroit) announces his plan to introduce community solar legislation focused on supporting low income households and organized labor. May 13, 2026 | Photo by Kyle Davidson/Michigan Advance

McKinney, who is also a candidate for Congress, told reporters the policy would ensure all subscribers receive at least 10% savings on their energy bill, with low-income households receiving deeper discounts - at least 20% savings. It would also ensure 40% of the capacity in a community solar program is reserved for low-income families and service organizations, he said.

The legislation ensures renters and condo owners can access these programs and supports local ownership, allowing schools, churches, farms, nonprofits and small businesses to own or subscribe to community solar programs, McKinney said.

The bill also requires utilities to connect community solar projects to their grid, McKinney said.

Additionally, any community solar projects more than 1 megawatt would be required to use project labor agreements, hire union apprentices and pay prevailing wages, McKinney said.

Village of L'Anse Community Solar ribbon cutting ceremony in September 2019. | Julie Staveland, EGLE via Flickr

Members of the Michigan Environmental Justice Coalition, the Sierra Club, and Vote Solar joined McKinney in announcing the bill, with the representative noting that he was working with the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers and the building trades to finalize bill language on labor standards.

While community solar legislation has held support from Democrats and Republicans, McKinney said influence from Michigan's energy utilities has been an obstacle, noting that he had also backed legislation to bar energy utilities from making political contributions.

Consumers Energy Director of Media Relations Katie Carey said the company does not plan to comment on the legislation until it has been introduced and the company has had the opportunity to review it.

DTE Spokesperson Ryan Lowry said the company is reviewing the proposal.

Link to the original story.

value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment
value.Overview: The Bay City Times: Web Edition (Michigan), ENVIRONMENT, 1568words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGX-T5S3-RS01-R0W0-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

value.Title: DTE Energy Company ( NYSE : DTE ) continues to outperform Exelon Corporation by 6.6% in the past three months

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-S553-S8GP-017Y-00000-00DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform Exelon Corporation by 6.6% in the past three months2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform Exelon Corporation by 6.6% in the past three months

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's (NYSE:DTE) share price is continuing to outperform that of Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC). DTE Energy Company's return over the past 3 months has been 2.0% and Exelon Corporation's return has been -4.6% over the same period.

Here is how DTE Energy Company and Exelon Corporation compare:

Company Name1 Month %3 Month%YTD %1 yr %DTE Energy Company-4.72.011.410.7Exelon Corporation-7.4-4.64.05.9

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Exelon Corporation operates as a leading regulated utility holding company providing electricity and natural gas to nearly 11 million residential, commercial, and industrial customers across its service territories. The core business consists of electricity transmission and distribution operations through established subsidiaries like ComEd, PECO, BGE, and PHI utilities. The largest revenue sources derive directly from electric delivery services and natural gas distribution, which benefit from approved distribution and transmission rates designed to enhance grid reliability. The fastest-growing segments include the distribution and transmission rate bases at ComEd, BGE, and PHI, driven by higher capital expenditures, favorable weather impacts, and targeted infrastructure investments. By balancing consistent capital deployment with cost management, the enterprise serves widespread end markets across multiple states with highly essential energy infrastructure services.

value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-47V3-SBS7-N133-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment
value.Overview: Ann Arbor News: Web Edition (Michigan), ENVIRONMENT, 1568words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGX-T5S3-RS01-R0SW-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment
value.Overview: Jackson Citizen Patriot: Web Edition (Michigan), ENVIRONMENT, 1568words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-47V3-SBS7-N0VT-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment
value.Overview: The Muskegon Chronicle: Web Edition (Michigan), ENVIRONMENT, 1568words

urn:contentItem:6JGX-T5S3-RS01-R13W-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."
