value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGX-T5S3-RS01-R124-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

urn:contentItem:6JGY-47V3-SBS7-N0SW-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case 2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTYears after sediment bomb, $25M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sedimentGarret Ellison

LANSING, MI -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay $25 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled on Friday, April 24, four days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgement on April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Departments of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy (EGLE) and Natural Resources (DNR).

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This $25 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

Halloween drawdown impact worsened over a year

The settlement comes roughly six-and-a-half years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019 to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say subsequently mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12-miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks, filled pools and river backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

More: 1967 Chevy Impala found in muddy lakebed

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic-yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" $10 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup. The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

'An unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are both located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under FERC oversight and relied on federally-approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a statement this week, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities. "The actions we took on October 31, 2019 saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, FERC spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

Drawdown had lasting impact

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows. Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100 percent certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," said Diana. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree. "We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered. But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," he said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the $25 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

value.Title: DTE Energy Company ( NYSE : DTE )- 5 Reasons Investors Remain Bullish (and 3 Reasons Why Bearish)

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-GFY3-RW84-10TR-00000-00DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE)- 5 Reasons Investors Remain Bullish (and 3 Reasons Why Bearish)2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE)- 5 Reasons Investors Remain Bullish (and 3 Reasons Why Bearish)

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) has climbed 9.9% Year To Date. There is uncertainty in the market and it is difficult to predict how economic conditions will be in 2026 and beyond as well as what the impact of those conditions may be on DTE Energy Company. However, there are several key reasons why some investors remain bullish on the long-term prospects for DTE Energy Company.

Below are some of those key reasons:

- The earnings yield of 4.6% is 1.1 times the 10-year bond yield of 4.35%.

- Return on Equity of 11.9% versus sector average of 9.9%.

- As per the Du Pont analysis, Return on Equity of 11.9% is better than sector average of 9.9%. This is computed as net profit margin of 9.2% times asset turnover [sales/assets] of 0.29 times leverage factor [total assets/shareholders' equity] of 4.4.

- LONG-TERM FUNDAMENTAL RANKING: 4 OUT OF 5 [5 is best]; The company paid a dividend of $3.42 in the last twelve months. The dividend yield is high at 3.2%. Finally, its earnings growth in the past 12 months has been a comparatively moderate 3.8%.

- Tobin's Q Ratio, defined as MCap divided by Total Assets, is 0.5. Compared with the rest of the market the stock is undervalued.

However, there are also some reasons investors may remain bearish in the long-term including the following:

- The Price to Book of 11.8 indicates overvaluation compared with sector average of 2.6 and 6.2 for the NYSE Market.

- Total Liabilities/EBITDA of 12.3 is more than 5, this compares unfavourably with the Joseph Piotroski benchmark of 5.

- Net profit margin has averaged 10.5% in the last 3 years. This is lower than the sector average of 14.2% and suggests a low margin of safety.

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

urn:contentItem:6JHV-K623-RSSV-Y0CF-00000-00Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDOMESTIC NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALAppeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suitKELLY HOUSE/Bridge MichiganBridge Michigan

A state appeals panel on Thursday upheld the bulk of Michigan’s regulations limiting local control over renewable energy projects, while rejecting narrow aspects that critics had decried as regulatory overreach.

The three-judge Michigan Court of Appeals panel ruled that state regulators followed proper legal processes when they set rules to carry out a controversial 2023 law that allows the Michigan Public Service Commission to approve large wind, solar and battery projects over local objections.

But judges ruled the commission interpreted certain aspects of that law in ways that improperly limited local power.

It wasn’t immediately clear how the split ruling could affect the multiple renewable energy development proposals currently awaiting state approval. However, state officials seemed to see it as a victory.

“While the Commission continues to review the impact of specific findings of the Court’s decision on cases before us, today’s decision largely affirms the Commission’s approach and allows for continued and timely implementation of the law,” said Matt Helms, a spokesperson for the Public Service Commission.

A lawyer for the dozens of local communities that had sued the state over its solar permitting rules called the ruling a “mixed bag.”

Attorney Michael Homier said he’s pleased with portions of the ruling that favored his clients, but “disappointed the court didn’t apply the same reasoning” to his clients’ other arguments.

The ruling followed a tense yearslong debate about Public Act 233, which passed along party lines in 2023 as Democrats sought to speed up a renewable energy transition that had been slowed in part by fierce local opposition to planned wind and solar arrays.

After the Michigan Public Service Commission wrote rules to carry out the law in 2024, dozens of local communities sued, arguing the rules undermine local control in ways the law never envisioned.

The law allows local governments to retain jurisdiction over renewable energy proposals so long as they enact a so-called “compatible renewable energy ordinance” containing terms no stricter than the new statewide standards governing noise, setbacks and other particulars.

If they don’t have such an ordinance, developers can instead seek approval from the Public Service Commission.

Among other things, the local government groups argued the commission failed to follow proper rulemaking procedure and wrote overly narrow terms for the local ordinances.

In their ruling Thursday, Judges Christopher Murray, Michael Gadola and Michael Kelly rejected those arguments and several others posed by the local governments.

But they sided with the governments on two issues:

1. The law gives local governments 30 days after a meeting with developers to start the local approval process. The state wrote rules that instead set the 30-day clock to start when developers offer to meet. Judges called that a misinterpretation of the law, rejecting state lawyers’ concerns that local governments may now be able to block projects simply by refusing to meet.

2. The justices agreed with the local governments that any county, township, city or village that touches project lands is considered an “affected local unit,” and thus eligible for certain privileges, including a payment from developers. The state had written rules saying only governments with zoning jurisdiction were considered an “affected local unit.”

In a press release, renewable energy advocates celebrated the ruling, with Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council President Laura Sherman saying it “affirmed the ability for Michiganders to use their land as they wish while stimulating job creation and economic development.”

Officials with the Michigan Townships Association, which has been critical of the state permitting system and lobbies on behalf of dozens of townships involved in the lawsuit, did not respond to a request for comment.

___

This story was originally published by Bridge Michigan and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 10-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms Exelon Corporation

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-DPW3-RXW9-W10C-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms Exelon Corporation2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms Exelon Corporation

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 7.4% annualized over 10 years.

In comparison, Exelon Corporation's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 4.2% annualized over 10 years.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $142.44 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.1. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.63 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Exelon Corporation's last trading price was $45.02 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 16.4. With an EPS growth rate of 9.6 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 1.71 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Exelon Corporation operates as a leading regulated utility holding company providing electricity and natural gas to nearly 11 million residential, commercial, and industrial customers across its service territories. The core business consists of electricity transmission and distribution operations through established subsidiaries like ComEd, PECO, BGE, and PHI utilities. The largest revenue sources derive directly from electric delivery services and natural gas distribution, which benefit from approved distribution and transmission rates designed to enhance grid reliability. The fastest-growing segments include the distribution and transmission rate bases at ComEd, BGE, and PHI, driven by higher capital expenditures, favorable weather impacts, and targeted infrastructure investments. By balancing consistent capital deployment with cost management, the enterprise serves widespread end markets across multiple states with highly essential energy infrastructure services.

urn:contentItem:6JHM-DPW3-RXW9-W1FR-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Lags WEC Energy Group, Inc2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 10-Year Earnings Growth Lags WEC Energy Group, Inc

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 7.4% annualized over 10 years.

In comparison, WEC Energy Group, Inc's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 7.5% annualized over 10 years.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $142.44 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.1. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.63 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

WEC Energy Group, Inc's last trading price was $113.90 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 23.4. With an EPS growth rate of 2.8 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 8.48 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

This increase comprises cumulative retained earnings (RETE) of $2.7 billion and Created Market Value of $19.9 billion. The Created Market Value multiple, defined by the change in MCap for every $1 of retained earnings is exemplary at $8.40.

value.Title: Here's how city could use 7.4M from property sale

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-P5P3-RTB0-B100-00000-00Here's how city could use 7.4M from property sale 2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisAPg. 002Here's how city could use 7.4M from property sale Ryan Stanton - ryanstanton@mlive.com

While once contemplated for affordable housing, Ann Arbor officials are now leaning toward selling the city-owned Kline's lot to a luxury housing developer.

And the sale of the downtown parking lot along Ashley Street is estimated to bring 7.4 million to the city's coffers.

How should it be spent?

City Administrator Milton Dohoney laid out his recommendations in an April 20 memo to City Council.

He proposed putting the biggest share, .6 million, into establishing a strategic property acquisition fund to become more proactive in protecting the city's tax base. City officials have talked about wanting to go after properties the University of Michigan otherwise might acquire and take off the tax rolls, so the city instead could flip them for redevelopment.

"We would acquire parcels for strategic reuse consistent with our interests," Dohoney wrote, explaining the city's economic development director would oversee the fund.

Another 00,000 is proposed for the economic development office to use for due diligence work, including legal services, when evaluating economic development opportunities.

Dohoney also proposed giving .5 million to the Ann Arbor Housing Commission, either going into a general affordable housing fund or earmarked for a specific project.

Another million could go toward the city's new sustainable energy utility, which is being set up to support local investments in solar and geothermal energy for residents and others.

"In working on the development of the utility, we have been made painfully aware that creating something with no assets is extraordinarily difficult," Dohoney wrote.

He proposed putting another million into the city's reserves for the city's financial stability, million toward purchase of a new records management system expected to help improve police transparency, million for continuation of Throne portable public restrooms downtown, million for Dixboro bridge repairs and 50,000 for adding 39 streetlights and replacing existing solar crossing lights on Ann Arbor-Saline Road.

Another 00,000 would go to CBRE, the city's real estate broker for the Kline's lot deal, which Dohoney said must be paid.

Remaining allocations proposed by the administrator include 00,000 for the Shelter Association ofWashtenaw County, which runs the Delonis Center homeless shelter in Ann Arbor, and 50,000 for Alpha House, which helps shelter families and children experiencing homelessness.

Dohoney said the money for Alpha House could go toward a new family shelter on the Trinity Health hospital campus, while the money for the Shelter Association could go toward helping the agency establish a second shelter somewhere in the county as the Delonis Center is beyond capacity.

"The need to provide services is steadily increasing and the administration has been in ongoing discussions on the best way to address the issue," he wrote. "The new leadership at the center has concluded that they need a second facility located somewhere within the county to continue their work."

City Council voted 10-0 in September to direct city staff to negotiate selling the Kline's lot to development firm Hines, which is proposing 100% luxury-branded apartments.

City Council hasn't yet made any final decisions about selling the lot or how to spend the proceeds.

value.Title: Hochul’s opposition to climate action simply ignores reality | Opinion
value.Overview: Lohud | The Journal News Online (New York), CLIMATE CHANGE NEWS & US NEWS, 1925words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-0FW3-RSRV-F18H-00000-00Hochul’s opposition to climate action simply ignores reality | Opinion2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisCLIMATE CHANGE NEWS & US NEWSHochul’s opposition to climate action simply ignores reality | OpinionMark Dunlea, Special to the USA TODAY Network

In a recent op-ed published by Empire Report — Advocates Opposing Climate Act Changes Ignoring Reality — Ken Lovett, a communications advisor to Gov. Kathy Hochul, continues the pattern of misleading and incomplete information promoted by her administration to defend her efforts to avoid complying with the Climate Leadership and Community Protection Act, or CLCPA.

While Lovett and Hochul defend her retreat on the grounds that the world has changed since the CLCPA was passed, they ignore that the biggest change is that global warming and extreme weather are continuing to accelerate, even faster than expected. Former President Joe Biden and Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont have repeatedly warned that climate change presents an existential threat to humanity. The Secretary-General of the United Nations has repeatedly warned that the slow climate efforts by governments have opened up the Gates to Hell.

New York's climate policy should be driven by science

Climate action should be a matter of science, not politics and election spin. Even the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, or IPCC, admits that its climate emission reduction goals — faster than the CLCPA — are too slow to avoid catastrophic climate change. To avoid that fate, the IPCC promotes carbon capture, even while admitting that it is not presently feasible. Climate activist Greta Thunberg chided them in her address to the UN for leaving the fate of her generation to the success of a Hail Mary pass.

Converting the state’s energy system from fossil fuels to clean renewable energy certainly poses challenges. The biggest challenge, however, is not technical but political. Meeting timelines requires coordinated planning and strong leadership at the top — both of which have been missing under Hochul.

Link to Image

While President Donald Trump certainly deserves blame for his war against clean renewable energy and his promotion of the fossil fuel industry and their high prices, New York’s failure to meet renewable energy goals stretches back decades, long before Trump. Gov. George Pataki, for instance, set goals in 2002 for renewable energy which the state’s failed to come close to meeting.

The CLPA, adopted in 2019, largely put into statue climate policies that had been official state policy since a 2009 gubernatorial executive order. Two hundred climate and community groups had supported a stronger bill that would have required a much stronger planning process while updating the state’s climate goals, including making them more aligned with the lower heating target developing nations had won in the Paris climate accords. It also had a much faster timeline with short-term benchmarks. To get legislators and the executive to act, you need 2-year goals and benchmarks, not 10-to-30-year milestones.

New York moves as slow as molasses. After the climate law passed, the state spent three years to develop a scoping plan for climate action. Even though the plan was largely written by the Governor’s office, Hochul has ignored many of its recommendations, including ignoring the legal mandate to incorporate it into the recent update to the state energy master plan.

The state’s climate scoping plan provided a low-ball estimate of needing $10 billion annually for 30 years to fund the transition to a renewable energy future. Lovett wants us to applaud Hochul for providing $1 billion — to be spent over five years — in last year’s budget for a Sustainable Future Fund, which represents 2% of what her administration previously said was needed.

Both plans were more of a college white paper, outlining key questions and visions rather than providing a detailed plan of action with budget, timelines, and intermediate goals. The governor, for instance, rammed in support for extremely expensive nuclear power plants contrary to the scoping plan. New York continues to provide more money to nuclear subsidies than it does to renewables, with Hochul recently adding $30 billion plus over two decades to the initiative started by Cuomo to keep four unprofitable, old, upstate nuclear reactors open that the owner wanted to close. Lawmakers should impose a two-year moratorium on nuclear to do a financial assessment while re-directly the $30 billion to renewable energy.

Another example of slow action is the issue of the state power plant that powers the Capitol and the Empire State Plaza in Albany. For more than a century, the plant has polluted Sheridan Hollow, a low-income community of color, by burning coal, oil, trash and now gas. Since 2017, climate and community groups have been calling to shut down the plant and use geothermal instead. The state finally did a study, which took two-plus years to complete and then proposed a 15-to-20-year timeline. Meanwhile, the state of Michigan took 18 months — from study to completion — to convert its state Capitol to geothermal.

A second study of the New York Capitol complex was done as part of a budget agreement to look at how to decarbonize the state’s 15 largest greenhouse gas emitters. Lawmakers had initially sought a concrete commitment to do the conversions. That two-year study was finally completed in January, but NYPA says it can’t release it until June— after the post-legislative session — since it argues it has to redact details about individual buildings.

Hochul recently stated that it would be foolish for anyone — including her and her staff — to imply that high utility bills in New York are due to the climate law giving the slowness of her administration in implementing it. States with lower electric costs also tend to be the ones with a higher percentage of their electricity coming from renewable energy.

As even the New York Independent System Operator has acknowledged, the main driver of high energy costs is reliance on natural gas and its volatile prices, a situation made worse by the present Trump-Netanyahu war on Iran. Continued investment in expensive gas pipelines also drives up costs. Hochul recently had the state reverse course and approve the Northeast Supply Enanchement gas pipeline under New Yorkharbor.

Solar is the cheapest source of electricity on the planet, though government red tape and bureaucracy in the Unted States has slowed down adoption compared to other countries such as those in Europe. In Bill McKibben’s new book, "Here Comes the Sun," he points out that everyday Pakistanis — with virtually no help from their government — have installed a massive number of cheap solar units from neighboring China to escape from the high cost and unreliability of the existing electric grid. In only six years, solar has gone from 0 to 30% of Pakistan’s energy supply. Texas makes it easier than New York to build renewable energy and leads the country in the development of solar, wind, and geothermal. California often meets 100% of its energy needs from renewables and battery storage for at least part of the day.

Besides more funding for solar, lawmakers should pass several bills — ASAP, SUNNY — to speed up solar development..

Our view: Climate-law fix will help New Yorkers with affordability | Editorial

Opinion: New York needs nuclear. Just look at our stretched grid | Opinion

Climate change is a public health crisis in New York

Lovett and Hochul ignore that massive costs and negative health impacts that New Yorkers are already experiencing from climate change. New Yorkers annually pay an estimated $50 billion in Increased health care costs due to air pollution. Flooding is an increasing problem, especially in New York City, with some even dying in basement apartments. Air quality has plummeted in recent summers due to wildfires in Canada. Extreme heat is the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the U.S and New York State, with more than 500 residents of New York City alone dying annually.

One way to achieve lower energy costs is through public power, as has been done in many states — though public control is also critical. Even in New York, we have seen this with the 50-plus municipally owned utilities plus the cheap hydropower from the New York Power Authority. Yet Hochul and the NYPA are resisting the direction by the state Legislature to build publicly owned renewables.

Lovett also trots out the much maligned — and indeed fraudulent — 3-page memo by NYSERDA that contends that the Governor’s cap-and-trade/invest proposal for carbon pricing “would cost upstate households more than $4,000 annually on their utility bills.” In order to come up with the $4,000 estimate, Hochul is using a price that is more than 800% higher than what she has been proposing, a fact not mentioned in the NYSERDA paper.

Carbon pricing — making fossil fuels pay for the damages their use causes to the rest of society — is viewed by economists as the single most effective way to reduce their burning and convert to renewable energy. While a price on carbon is intended to raise the price of fossil fuels in order to reduce the use, rebating part or all of the revenue reduces or eliminates the negative impact on people’s wallets. Hochul has proposed a 33% rebate. A carbon tax bill that I drafted a decade ago along with some economic professors had a rebate of 60%, targeted to low- and middle-income New Yorkers which would have held them harmless. Some groups prefer a 100% rebate. (And perhaps the only positive thing from COVID-19 is that the state now knows how to send checks to individual New Yorkers without relying on tax filings to do that.)

Most climate groups prefer a carbon tax approach instead. Environmental justice — and even Pope Francis — have long opposed the cap-and-trade approach promoted by Hochul as hurting such communities while being subject to Wall Street speculation. Interestingly, both fossil fuel companies and senior Republicans — not Trump — have long supported a carbon tax, though they want it to be national for standardization and with the poison bill of absolving them of any legal liability for knowingly causing climate change.

The state’s climate law does not require a price on carbon. It does require a plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, a deadline which Hochul missed. Hochul chose cap-and-trade to do this. One problem with this approach is that in the last moments before the plan is formally adopted, politicians invariably weaken the targets to appease the fossil fuel industry. In this case, Hochul is seeking to weaken it much earlier in the process.

In 2010, the Green Party first proposed a Green New Deal, which combined a ten-year timeline to 100% renewables and zero emissions with an economic bill of rights that inlcuded living-wage jobs, universal health care, and education. A key point is that we need a just transition, that guarantees a decent standard of living for everyone.

With Trump installed as climate-denier-in-chief, it is critical that state governments step up to take effective climate action. Hochul unfortunately is not the only Democratic governor that is responding to high energy prices — caused by excessive profits and exploitation of the fossil fuel industry and utilities — by backing away from existing climate goals. Fortunately, many state lawmakers, especially from downstate, have been resisting so far. For the sake of our planet, our grandchildren, and ourselves, state lawmakers need to say no to Hochul and redouble efforts to speed up climate action.

Mark Dunlea is author of "Putting Out the Planetary Fire."

This article originally appeared on Rockland/Westchester Journal News: Hochul’s opposition to climate action simply ignores reality | Opinion

value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 3-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms WEC Energy Group, Inc

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-V373-RT96-N07G-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms WEC Energy Group, Inc2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms WEC Energy Group, Inc

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 8.4% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 41.5% or 14.9% annualized.

In comparison, WEC Energy Group, Inc's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 2.8% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 35.1% or 13.4% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $146.73 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 20.8. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.49 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

WEC Energy Group, Inc's last trading price was $116.44 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 24. With an EPS growth rate of 2.8 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 8.67 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

value.Title: DTE Energy 's dividend in perspective: comparison with NiSource Inc. *Updated*

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-V373-RT96-N0KD-00000-00DTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with NiSource Inc. *Updated*2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with NiSource Inc. *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $4.51. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.97%. Its current price is at a 1.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 21.1% premium to its 52 week low. If DTE Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.6%.

In comparison, NiSource Inc.'s trailing twelve months dividend is $1.16. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.4%. Its current price is at a 1.3% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 4.5% premium to its 52 week low. If NiSource Inc. was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 2.51%.

$1000 invested into DTE Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,679 today. This total gain of $679 is generated by a capital gain of $456 and dividends of $223.

$1000 invested into NiSource Inc. stock 5 years ago would be worth $2,589 today. This total gain of $1,589 is generated by a capital gain of $1,209 and dividends of $380.

DTE Energy's dividend has grown 7.1% over the last 4 years.

NiSource Inc.'s dividend has grown 6.1% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

DTE Energy, headquartered in Detroit, operates as a diversified energy company with its primary focus on regulated electric and natural gas utilities. The company serves 2.3 million electric customers in Southeast Michigan and 1.4 million gas customers statewide, making these utilities its largest revenue sources. Its portfolio also includes non-utility businesses specializing in custom energy solutions, renewable energy generation, and energy marketing and trading. The fastest-growing segments include clean energy development, such as solar parks under the MIGreenPower program, and hyperscale data center contracts like the 1.4 GW Oracle agreement. These initiatives drive economic progress, affordability benefits, and advancements toward net-zero goals.

urn:contentItem:6JH5-ND63-SC57-30N3-00000-00DTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with CMS Energy Corporation *Updated*2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with CMS Energy Corporation *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $4.51. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.97%. Its current price is at a 1.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 21.1% premium to its 52 week low. If DTE Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.6%.

In comparison, CMS Energy Corporation's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.20. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.86%. Its current price is at a 4.5% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 15.9% premium to its 52 week low. If CMS Energy Corporation was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.32%.

$1000 invested into DTE Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,679 today. This total gain of $679 is generated by a capital gain of $456 and dividends of $223.

$1000 invested into CMS Energy Corporation stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,612 today. This total gain of $612 is generated by a capital gain of $398 and dividends of $213.

DTE Energy's dividend has grown 7.1% over the last 4 years.

CMS Energy Corporation's dividend has grown 5.6% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

DTE Energy, headquartered in Detroit, operates as a diversified energy company with its primary focus on regulated electric and natural gas utilities. The company serves 2.3 million electric customers in Southeast Michigan and 1.4 million gas customers statewide, making these utilities its largest revenue sources. Its portfolio also includes non-utility businesses specializing in custom energy solutions, renewable energy generation, and energy marketing and trading. The fastest-growing segments include clean energy development, such as solar parks under the MIGreenPower program, and hyperscale data center contracts like the 1.4 GW Oracle agreement. These initiatives drive economic progress, affordability benefits, and advancements toward net-zero goals.

urn:contentItem:6JGY-VSX3-S55N-04W7-00000-00DTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Consolidated Edison, Inc. *Updated*2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Consolidated Edison, Inc. *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $4.51. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.97%. Its current price is at a 1.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 21.1% premium to its 52 week low. If DTE Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.6%.

In comparison, Consolidated Edison, Inc.'s trailing twelve months dividend is $3.44. Its dividend yield at its current price is 3.08%. Its current price is at a 4.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 18.9% premium to its 52 week low. If Consolidated Edison, Inc. was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.67%.

$1000 invested into DTE Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,679 today. This total gain of $679 is generated by a capital gain of $456 and dividends of $223.

$1000 invested into Consolidated Edison, Inc. stock 5 years ago would be worth $2,031 today. This total gain of $1,031 is generated by a capital gain of $733 and dividends of $298.

DTE Energy's dividend has grown 7.1% over the last 4 years.

Consolidated Edison, Inc.'s dividend has grown 2.6% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

DTE Energy, headquartered in Detroit, operates as a diversified energy company with its primary focus on regulated electric and natural gas utilities. The company serves 2.3 million electric customers in Southeast Michigan and 1.4 million gas customers statewide, making these utilities its largest revenue sources. Its portfolio also includes non-utility businesses specializing in custom energy solutions, renewable energy generation, and energy marketing and trading. The fastest-growing segments include clean energy development, such as solar parks under the MIGreenPower program, and hyperscale data center contracts like the 1.4 GW Oracle agreement. These initiatives drive economic progress, affordability benefits, and advancements toward net-zero goals.

value.Title: Land Commissioner: Matthew McQueen (D)

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGH-85N3-S415-P1W3-00000-00Land Commissioner: Matthew McQueen (D)2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisELECTIONLand Commissioner: Matthew McQueen (D)

Name: Matthew McQueen

Political Party: Democratic

Age: 58

Education: BA, Williams College; JD, University of Michigan Law School; MS, University of Michigan School of Natural Resources; MBA, University of New Mexico

Occupation: Attorney

City of Residence: Galisteo

Campaign Website: www.mcqueenfornm.com

How has your background prepared you to serve as New Mexico's land commissioner?

With over 30 years' experience practicing real property law (specializing in land conservation), 12 years in the Legislature, and 10 years as Chair of the House Energy, Environment & Natural Resources Committee, everything in my personal, professional, and political background has prepared me to serve as New Mexico's Land Commissioner.

If elected, how would you seek to maximize revenue for schools, universities and other state trust land beneficiaries?

The State Land Office earns over $2 Billion a year in revenue, but 97% of that revenue comes from oil and gas. The Land Office must diversify its revenue, with more renewable projects (wind, solar, geothermal), commercial projects (on appropriate parcels), and housing projects (again, on appropriate parcels).

Would you support or oppose efforts to expand renewable energy installations on state trust land?

Absolutely support. It's one of the ways to diversify the revenue of the Land Office, and it builds towards the coming energy transition away from fossil fuels. New Mexico is an energy producing state, and with its substantial wind, solar, and geothermal resources, it will be in the future.

Given the legacy of Jeffrey Epstein's Zorro Ranch in New Mexico, would you propose any changes to how the State Land Office handles and regulates applications for grazing leases?

The State Land Office has already started requiring additional information from potential lessees about the actual, beneficial ownersnot merely anonymous, closely-held corporations or LLCs. I will draw on my professional background as an attorney and build on that practice.

Do you support or oppose the state's current oil and gas royalty rate structure? If elected, would you seek to make any changes to the current royalty rates?

In the Legislature I worked to increase the maximum royalty rate from 20% to 25% (Senate Bill 23, 2025). This rate better reflects fair market value and will increase payments to the Land Grant Permanent Fund by $1 Billion over the life of the bill. I support this rate structure.

What would your approach to potential land swaps be? In addition, what criteria would you use to evaluate such transactions?

Land swaps are an important tool to deal with New Mexico's checkerboarded and fragmented landscape. Land swaps must, of course, be a net benefit to the state, not sacrifice public access for outdoor recreation (including hunting and fishing), and have a robust public review and input process.

Have you or your business, if you are a business owner, ever been the subject of any state or federal tax liens?

Yes. In 2022 I failed to file the proper form with the Department of Workforce Solutions and ended up with a $172 business lien. (It was quickly paid.)

Have you ever been arrested for, charged with, or convicted of drunken driving, any misdemeanor or any felony in New Mexico or any other state? If so, explain.

No.

Have you ever been involved in a personal or business bankruptcy proceeding?

No.

Challengers

Land Commissioner: Jonas Moya (D)

Land Commissioner: Juan Sanchez (D)

value.Title: DTE Energy 's dividend in perspective: comparison with Nextera Energy, Inc. *Updated*

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGB-8C73-RSVS-J48B-00000-00DTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Nextera Energy, Inc. *Updated*2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisDTE Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Nextera Energy, Inc. *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $4.51. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.97%. Its current price is at a 1.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 21.1% premium to its 52 week low. If DTE Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.6%.

In comparison, Nextera Energy, Inc.'s trailing twelve months dividend is $2.32. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.37%. Its current price is at a 0.2% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 57.8% premium to its 52 week low. If Nextera Energy, Inc. was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.74%.

$1000 invested into DTE Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,679 today. This total gain of $679 is generated by a capital gain of $456 and dividends of $223.

$1000 invested into Nextera Energy, Inc. stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,607 today. This total gain of $607 is generated by a capital gain of $442 and dividends of $164.

DTE Energy's dividend has grown 7.1% over the last 4 years.

Nextera Energy, Inc.'s dividend has grown 10.1% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

DTE Energy, headquartered in Detroit, operates as a diversified energy company with its primary focus on regulated electric and natural gas utilities. The company serves 2.3 million electric customers in Southeast Michigan and 1.4 million gas customers statewide, making these utilities its largest revenue sources. Its portfolio also includes non-utility businesses specializing in custom energy solutions, renewable energy generation, and energy marketing and trading. The fastest-growing segments include clean energy development, such as solar parks under the MIGreenPower program, and hyperscale data center contracts like the 1.4 GW Oracle agreement. These initiatives drive economic progress, affordability benefits, and advancements toward net-zero goals.

value.Title: The Dam Dilemma: How To Build Dams To Best Protect Community, Environment

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-RSC3-RSH7-11WV-00000-00The Dam Dilemma: How To Build Dams To Best Protect Community, Environment2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisThe Dam Dilemma: How To Build Dams To Best Protect Community, EnvironmentEurasia Review

As the U.S. and other countries expand clean energy, large hydropower dams, often seen as a reliable renewable solution, can come with significant environmental and social costs if not planned thoughtfully.

New research from Michigan State University finds that without better planning and community involvement, dam projects risk repeating decades-old mistakes — raising important questions about dam construction and hydroelectricity projects.

Today, hydropower generates more than half of the world’s renewable electricity, and the research team’s findings, published in the journal Nature Sustainability, are starting to form the basis for global conversations on how to address the challenges surrounding dam projects.

“Hydropower has been sold as a solution to a country’s energy needs but our studies show that, in most cases, countries building large-scale hydropower forget to ensure that damages are minimized in local communities,” said Emilio Moran, an MSU John A. Hannah Distinguished Professor in the Department of Geography, Environment and Spatial Sciences. “Large dams bring about irreversible damages to river ecosystems, fisheries and fishermen’s livelihoods.”

Background and history

In the early 2000s, the World Commission on Dams outlined strategies to reduce environmental and social impacts, but the report was dismissed by the major dam-building nations such as China, India and Brazil, who claimed that the recommendations would be impractical and slow economic development goals.

A quarter century later, as problems persist, the research team sought to understand what characterizes the current wave of large hydropower dam building in the global south.

That said, a growing number of local communities are organizing internationally to protect their environments and livelihoods, with questions around policy and sustainability becoming more important for future projects.

Findings and implications

To help steer hydropower projects toward a more sustainable path, instead of building dams project by project, the study suggests looking at entire river systems and incorporating local communities, government entities and energy providers at the start of decision-making.

This approach would help identify overlooked opportunities, including:

Reduce environmental impacts

Better adapting to the effects of climate change

Incorporate other aspects of hydropower to include solar, wind, hydrokinetic turbines, and floating solar panels in the energy mix of dam building.

While these technologies are already proven to increase renewable energy production and improve resilience, the researchers call for more use and funding.

The study suggests there has been a growing call to move away from top-down decision-making to a more collaborative, cross-sector approach known as horizontal governance, where multiple groups share responsibility, including an increasingly popular “prosumer” model, where users both consume and produce energy.

“Such a model would empower local and Indigenous communities to become both producers and consumers of their own energy,” said Sergio Villamayor-Tomas, researcher at the Autonomous University of Barcelona and co-author of the study. “This approach can help to democratize the power system and ensure that those most affected by energy projects are also able to benefit from them, which generally has not been the case to date.”

While some countries look to hydropower as way to reduce fossil fuel emissions, the study demonstrates that renewable energy does not automatically equal sustainability; rather, it is a unique opportunity to encourage local energy decision-making and reduce dependence on fossil fuels.

“However, with more strategic planning, horizontal governance and community-led solutions, our work demonstrates that the tools for a better path forward already exist,” said co-author Maria Claudia Lopez, associate professor at MSU’s College of Agriculture and Natural Resources. “The question is not whether governments and investors will meet their energy needs, but [will they] do so without sacrificing the world’s vital ecosystems. The tools and frameworks are in place, and this article is one important contribution highlighting them and advancing ongoing discussions of the energy transition.”

value.Title: Court of Appeals Ensures Michigan Public Service Commission Can Continue to Implement Clean Energy and Jobs Act

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-J1T3-S5N7-F4FC-00000-00Court of Appeals Ensures Michigan Public Service Commission Can Continue to Implement Clean Energy and Jobs Act2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisCourt of Appeals Ensures Michigan Public Service Commission Can Continue to Implement Clean Energy and Jobs Act

Washington, DC: American Clean Power Association has issued the following press release:

The Michigan Court of Appeals ruled today in support of key parts of the state ’ s implementation of the Clean Energy and Jobs Act. The decision follows April 15 oral arguments in which the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) defended its ability to implement the siting and permitting provisions of Public Act 233 (PA 233) and pointed to the clear intent of the legislature and the governor in passing and signing the measure.

As intervening parties in the case, the Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council (Michigan EIBC), Institute for Energy Innovation, Clean Grid Alliance, and Advanced Energy United also participated in oral arguments, pointing specifically to the importance of PA 233 in providing legal certainty and stability for developers, investors, and communities by having a path to obtaining a permit.

In addition to materials submitted by the trade organizations, a broad set of business groups, renewable energy advocates, labor organizations, legislators, and local landowners filed amicus curiae brief petitions in support of the Commission ’ s actions to fully implement the permitting process established under PA 233. This law plays a crucial role in advancing Michigan ’ s clean energy future, ability to meet accelerating energy demand, and economic growth.

PA 233 strikes a careful balance between landowner rights and thorough project review. It ensures farmers can use their own land for wind and solar generation and energy storage, helping support their families, while requiring these projects to be carefully examined before permission is granted. The law was designed to create a statewide, fair siting and permitting process for renewable energy projects, creating a clear, predictable framework that ensures policies are consistent across the state, with the goal of attracting significant business investment, creating jobs, and positioning Michigan as a national leader in the clean energy sector.

Most importantly, the Court today upheld the MPSC ’ s authority to implement PA 233. It also upheld the MPSC ’ s definition of a “Compatible Renewable Energy Ordinance,” providing clarity to landowners, local governments, and developers.

Here are responses from the industry organization leaders:

“The Court of Appeals affirmed the ability for Michiganders to use their land as they wish while stimulating job creation and economic development,” said Dr. Laura Sherman, President of Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council and Institute for Energy Innovation. “We expect to remain engaged in this legal process to ensure that Michigan remains a national leader in the clean energy industry, while protecting property rights and supporting local communities. ”

“The passage of PA 233 demonstrated the state ’ s intent to remain a top destination for investment and innovation in wind, solar, and battery storage projects while protecting landowner rights and infusing much-needed revenue into communities,” said Elizabeth Wheeler, Senior Counsel and Director, Regulatory Advocacy, Clean Grid Alliance. “The Court ’ s ruling allows Michigan to move forward on this commitment. ”

“Upholding the MPSC ’ s siting and permitting authority through the Clean Energy and Jobs Act is critical to ensuring Michigan can meet its growing energy needs with more affordable in-state generation,” said John Albers, Regulatory Director for Advanced Energy United. “Following the legislature ’ s clear will and executing the law as intended promotes investment and creates jobs in Michigan, as well as bolsters landowner rights. ”

“PA 233 positions Michigan to compete for new energy investment at a time when demand for electricity is rising at historic levels,” said Erika Kowall, Director of Midwest State Affairs for the American Clean Power Association. “A more predictable siting supports faster deployment of new generation, strengthens grid reliability, and helps keep costs low for families and businesses. ”

value.Title: As 1,900-acre solar farm looms, Zeeland Township targets $100,000 toward legal fight Zeeland Township trustees on April 29 approved a $100,000 pot to pay for legal fees to fight a 1,900-acre solar farm proposal.
value.Overview: The Grand Rapids Press: Web Edition (Michigan), NEWS, 322words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-1033-RRVJ-M05V-00000-00As 1,900-acre solar farm looms, Zeeland Township targets $100,000 toward legal fight 2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisNEWSAs 1,900-acre solar farm looms, Zeeland Township targets $100,000 toward legal fight Zeeland Township trustees on April 29 approved a $100,000 pot to pay for legal fees to fight a 1,900-acre solar farm proposal.John Tunison

ZEELAND, MI -- As the prospect for a 1,900-acre solar farm looms for a rural Ottawa County township, local leaders have targeted an initial $100,000 to help fight the project.

Zeeland Township trustees on April 29 approved the funds.

They expect funds will be needed as RWE, the Germany-based energy company behind the 200 MW project, looks to potentially bypass the township permitting process and instead seek state approval.

Public sentiment toward the $300 million development, known as "Silver Maple Solar," has been decidedly negative since information about it first surfaced in summer 2025.

Residents have packed several meetings, raising concerns about water runoff, environmental issues, aesthetics and loss of prime farmland.

RWE and other developers of renewable energy projects have a right to bypass local government approval through Public Act 233 of 2023.

Under that process, the Michigan Public Service Commission would have the final say and not Zeeland Township.

The state process likely will be a lengthy one.

An administrative law judge will look at briefs submitted by both sides, followed by an in-person hearing to question statements from experts.

The next step in the state process is a June 4 "pre-hearing" conference.

As part of the April 29 meeting, Zeeland Township trustees also passed a resolution to officially oppose the proposed solar farm.

In the resolution, trustees cited the community opposition and the importance of agriculture to the "history, culture and economic vitality" of the township.

As part of requirements to Act 233, RWE has provided $75,000 to the township to help cover costs of participating in the MPSC process.

RWE, while pursuing the state approval route, also intends to continue efforts to obtain a township permit.

Township planning commissioners now are in the process of crafting a renewable energy ordinance to govern projects involving wind, solar and battery storage.
