value.Title: Michigan House advances GOP energy reform package
value.Overview: The Center Square, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, Coal industry news, US news, US politics news & US STATE GOVERNMENT NEWS, 944words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-M4G3-RRYS-M1G0-00000-00Michigan House advances GOP energy reform package2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, Coal industry news, US news, US politics news & US STATE GOVERNMENT NEWSMichigan House advances GOP energy reform packageBy Elyse Apel | The Center Square

(The Center Square) - Michigan House Republicans successfully advanced a major overhaul of the state's energy policies they say will lower utility bills and improve grid reliability.

The Republican-led legislation, House Bills 5710-5711, passed the House this week and now heads to the Democrat-held Senate.

State Rep. Ann Bollin, R-Brighton Township, said Michigan residents are struggling under rising utility bills and this package brings immediate relief.

"Families are telling me they are doing everything right and still falling behind because of higher utility costs," Bollin explained following the vote. "When electric bills keep climbing month after month, it creates real stress for parents, seniors living on fixed incomes, and hardworking people trying to make ends meet. Michigan families need some relief."

Bollin said the legislation would help ensure residents can rely on power during extreme weather while reducing costs.

"People should not have to worry about whether they can afford to keep the heat on in the winter or the air conditioning running during dangerous summer temperatures," Bollin said.

State Rep. Ron Robinson, R-Utica, described the package as a "top-to-bottom reset" of Michigan energy policy.

"We are righting the ship," Robinson said. "This is a serious, top-to-bottom reset of Michigan's energy policy that puts affordability and reliability first for families, workers, and job providers."

The bills would:

· Requires the Michigan Public Service Commission to prioritize affordability and reliability

· Stops funding to outside political activist groups

· Streamlines the process for building and upgrading energy infrastructure

· Increases transparency around ratehikes

These bills come just a couple years after Gov. Gretchen Whitmer joined fellow Democrats to pass a series of public acts cementing the state's clean energy strategy.

Among other provisions, the legislation established a new statewide standard requiring utilities to generate 80% of their electricity from clean sources by 2035 and reach 100% clean energy by 2040.

This new legislation is part of a broader House Republican affordability agenda that lawmakers say could save Michigan families roughly $1,400 annually through a combination of utility and property tax relief.

Environmental groups and clean energy advocates sharply criticized the proposals, arguing the bills would eliminate some of the state's most effective cost-saving energy policies.

Ben Poulson, state government affairs director for the Michigan League of Conservation Voters, told The Center Square that the reforms would make it harder to expand low-cost renewable energy and energy efficiency programs.

"It's disappointing that at a time when Michiganders are already struggling with rising energy costs, the House has chosen to pass reforms that make it harder to deploy some of the cheapest and cleanest energy resources available to Michigan families and businesses," Poulson said. "These proposals move us in the wrong direction at a moment when people are looking for real solutions to lower their bills and strengthen our energy future."

Poulson pointed to estimates that project billions in future savings if the current energy programs stay in place.

"Repealing clean energy targets will only make matters worse, as research shows it could instead lead to $5.6 billion in higher electricity costs statewide between 2026 and 2050," Poulson said. "At a time when energy costs are already rising, rolling back some of the most effective tools we have to reduce energy bills is deeply concerning."

He also argued the legislation would create additional barriers for homeowners seeking to install rooftop solar or other distributed energy systems.

"This legislation also makes it more difficult for individuals to take control of their own energy future," Poulson said. "By weakening requirements around connecting distributed energy resources like rooftop solar, it creates additional barriers for Michiganders who want to invest in their homes, lower their bills, and contribute to a more resilient energy system."

Supporters of the legislation reject claims the reforms would raise costs.

David Stevenson, director of energy and environmental policy at the Mackinac Center for Public Policy, told The Center Square that Michigan's current clean energy mandates are driving up utility bills by pushing utilities toward intermittent power sources.

"The reforms are exactly what Michigan needs to reduce energy costs and ensure reliability by allowing dependable energy plants to operate while removing mandates that favor intermittent, costly energy sources like wind and solar," Stevenson said.

Stevenson cited a Mackinac Center study projecting utility bills could rise significantly if Michigan continues transitioning away from coal and nuclear power.

"A Mackinac Center study projects that transitioning from dependable resources like coal and nuclear to solar and wind could dramatically increase ratepayers utility bills by more than $2,000 a year in the coming years," Stevenson said.

He added that renewable energy sources currently provide a relatively small share of Michigan's electricity generation and argued "dependable resources like coal and nuclear" remain necessary during periods of high demand.

"Wind and solar energy don't meet Michigan's energy needs in all seasons," Stevenson said. "Today, solar power provides under 3% of Michigan's electricity and wind under 8%, and those limited contributions show why dependable energy is essential. Dependable energy sources remain essential for affordability and reliability."

Both bills passed with only Republican support in the House.

value.Title: Landmark Michigan Appellate Decision Upholds Limits on Local Regulation of Renewable Energy Projects

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-YGM3-RYR5-84D2-00000-00Landmark Michigan Appellate Decision Upholds Limits on Local Regulation of Renewable Energy Projects2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisLandmark Michigan Appellate Decision Upholds Limits on Local Regulation of Renewable Energy Projects

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

ArentFox Schiff Amy Antoniolli Meera Gorjala Robert Middleton Duncan Weinstein

May 13th, 2026 ( JD Supra - Delivered by Newstex )

In a recent win for renewable energy developers, the Michigan Court of Appeals confirmed that Michigan law prohibits local governments from imposing requirements on wind, solar, and energy storage projects more restrictive than state law.

The decision comes on the heels of a similar appellate decision in Illinois upholding another 'no more restrictive than' state-level zoning structure. The Michigan decision largely affirms the Michigan Public Service Commission's (PSC) interpretation of a 2023 state law, effective November 29, 2024, that restructured the local approval process for renewable energy developments. The ruling provides important clarity for local governments and renewable energy developers planning projects.

Background

Enacted in 2023, Michigan Public Act 233 (PA 233) transformed siting for solar energy, wind energy, and energy storage systems. The law does not completely eliminate local zoning but shifts authority from local governments to the state by allowing developers to obtain a siting certificate from the PSC if projects meet certain capacity thresholds.

The law authorizes local governments to continue to regulate siting for qualifying projects only if the local government adopts an ordinance compatible with the new law. In practice, this means that local governments cannot adopt requirements more restrictive than state law standards. If an applicant seeks a certificate from the PSC and meets the statutory criteria, the PSC 'shall grant' approval. A certificate from the PSC preempts local siting law, though projects must still comply with local building or operating permit requirements.

The Lawsuit

A coalition of 79 townships and counties filed suit, arguing that the PSC's regulations implementing the new law exceeded its authority by redefining statutory terms, creating a new category of facilities, modifying statutory timelines, and implementing a rule without proper administrative rulemaking procedures. The local governments argued that their own siting ordinances could still include requirements that did not overlap with state law requirements.

The Court's Decision

The court upheld the PSC's interpretation that local siting ordinances 'may only contain the setback, fencing, height, sound, and other applicable requirements expressly outlined in [Michigan Code of Laws 460.1226(8)] and may not contain additional requirements more restrictive than those specifically identified in that section.' The court reasoned that allowing local governments to adopt requirements in addition to those imposed under state law 'would inherently be more restrictive,' which would conflict with the legislature's intent.

Key Takeaways

Local ordinances in Michigan cannot exceed state standards. The court affirmed that local governments cannot impose requirements more restrictive than those under state law. If a local government attempts to do so, developers may seek a PSC certificate as an alternative.

This is a growing trend. Like Michigan, an Illinois appellate court recently affirmed that a 2023 Illinois law requires counties to approve siting requests for solar projects that meet state law requirements, precluding counties from imposing more restrictive requirements.

[ View source.]

value.Title: Michigan families can't afford Trump's energy agenda

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK1-MGX3-RSP8-R1T4-00000-00Michigan families can't afford Trump's energy agenda2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisMichigan families can't afford Trump's energy agenda

Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Sergio Cira-Reyes

May 13th, 2026 ( Michigan Advance - Delivered by Newstex )

Electric meters. | Sarah Vogelsong/States Newsroom

Across the country, ratepayers are facing an influx of utility rate increases causing our energy bills to skyrocket as the costs of basic necessities continue to climb.

People are being forced to make dire choices between keeping the lights on and keeping food on the table. As a community organizer in Michigan who fights for affordability and a sustainable future, I know that reducing our energy bills is an urgent matter that must be given the attention it demands. The severity of this crisis cannot be overstated, and millions of Americans' quality of life is at stake.

Unfortunately, politicians in Washington are making a bad situation worse. The Trump administration has launched a dangerous onslaught against renewables by slashing clean energy tax credits in the Inflation Reduction Act that would have gotten more affordable energy on the grid, all while reviving expensive, inefficient coal plants and abusing federal powers to appease his fossil fuel industry donors.

Now, President Donald Trump has raised the stakes even higher: his latest 2027 budget proposes eliminating the Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP), a critical energy bill assistance program that provides direct support to those most impacted by the very crisis he is accelerating.

GET THE MORNING HEADLINES.

SUBSCRIBE

Right now, nearly 1 in 6 American households are behind on their utility bills. The rising cost of living is the number one issue for Michiganders right now, and our president and elected officials must take action to resolve the crisis - not rip away the existing safety nets meant to provide some relief. The country needs programs like LIHEAP now more than ever. To eliminate it while Americans are already struggling with energy bills and inflation will put families in an impossible situation with no clear way out.

LIHEAP extends a lifeline to over 430,000 Michiganders and five million Americans, including people with disabilities, families, seniors and households with high energy burdens, with both seasonal and year-round support. The program assists recipients in response to heating in colder regions to cooling in warmer regions and extreme weather events across the country. Every recipient's circumstances are different, but providing this assistance helps our most vulnerable neighbors stay afloat and reduces existing or potential debt.

The need to protect LIHEAP couldn't be more apparent in Michigan, as the Michigan Public Service Commission greenlit two hefty rate hikes for our state's largest utilities. DTE customers here in Detroit face a $242.4 million increase, and Consumers Energy customers face a $276 million increase (its largest increase in over 20 years). After their landmark rate case was approved, Consumers Energy already filed a notice for another rate increase, which if approved will mean even higher bills.Consumers Energy has said closing the J.H. Campbell plant was expected to save Michigan ratepayers $600 million by 2040. (Photo/Sarah Leach)

While Michiganders face these rate increases, the J.H. Campbell Coal Plant has been forced to delay its planned retirement four times due to Trump's Department of Energy invoking the Federal Power Act and mandating crumbling coal plants stay operational. Consumers Energy even weighed in that they wanted to close the plant due to its rising maintenance costs, and had even secured cheaper energy sources to safely plan for its retirement.

But now, keeping this expensive, dirty coal plant online has cost the utility over $600,000 per day, which consumers across the region will be paying for.

Trump's pledge to protect old coal plants makes even less sense for anyone who cares about lowering energy bills when coal is nearly 30% more expensive than it was just five years ago, while newly commissioned renewable energy projects have become increasingly affordable and quick to deploy.

Trump's intention to eliminate LIHEAP while pushing expensive fossil fuels onto the grid could not come at a worse time. He's cutting the safety net while making the fall even steeper for our families. Upon taking office, Trump promised to cut Americans' bills in half, lower gas prices and make life more affordable - but he's doing the opposite.

Rate increases like those we face here in Michigan are on the rise across the country, and until Americans are provided relief from costs that continue to climb-which are driven in part by continuing to rely on fossil fuels- we need to fund programs like LIHEAP. I have witnessed firsthand how working people benefit from these programs in my community.

As the Trump administration makes life harder by pushing costly energy, the least our leaders can do is make sure the families that are at risk of getting left behind get the support we need.

Link to the original story.

value.Title: The dam dilemma: How to build dams to best protect community, environment

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-MTF3-S08H-H3XY-00000-00The dam dilemma: How to build dams to best protect community, environment2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:50ZLexisNexisThe dam dilemma: How to build dams to best protect community, environmentTargeted News ServiceEAST LANSING, Michigan

Michigan State University posted the following news:

* * *

The dam dilemma: How to build dams to best protect community, environment

*

As the U.S. and other countries expand clean energy, large hydropower dams, often seen as a reliable renewable solution, can come with significant environmental and social costs if not planned thoughtfully.

New research from Michigan State University finds that without better planning and community involvement, dam projects risk repeating decades-old mistakes -raising important questions about dam construction and hydroelectricity projects.

Today, hydropower generates more than half of the world's renewable electricity, and the research team's findings, published in the journal Nature Sustainability, are starting to form the basis for global conversations on how to address the challenges surrounding dam projects.

"Hydropower has been sold as a solution to a country's energy needs but our studies show that, in most cases, countries building large-scale hydropower forget to ensure that damages are minimized in local communities," said Emilio Moran, an MSU John A. Hannah Distinguished Professor in the Department of Geography, Environment and Spatial Sciences. "Large dams bring about irreversible damages to river ecosystems, fisheries and fishermen's livelihoods."

Background and history

In the early 2000s, the World Commission on Dams outlined strategies to reduce environmental and social impacts, but the report was dismissed by the major dam-building nations such as China, India and Brazil, who claimed that the recommendations would be impractical and slow economic development goals.

A quarter century later, as problems persist, the research team sought to understand what characterizes the current wave of large hydropower dam building in the global south.

That said, a growing number of local communities are organizing internationally to protect their environments and livelihoods, with questions around policy and sustainability becoming more important for future projects.

Findings and implications

To help steer hydropower projects toward a more sustainable path, instead of building dams project by project, the study suggests looking at entire river systems and incorporating local communities, government entities and energy providers at the start of decision-making.

This approach would help identify overlooked opportunities, including:

* Reduce environmental impacts

* Better adapting to the effects of climate change

* Incorporate other aspects of hydropower to include solar, wind, hydrokinetic turbines, and floating solar panels in the energy mix of dam building.

While these technologies are already proven to increase renewable energy production and improve resilience, the researchers call for more use and funding.

The study suggests there has been a growing call to move away from top-down decision-making to a more collaborative, cross-sector approach known as horizontal governance, where multiple groups share responsibility, including an increasingly popular "prosumer" model, where users both consume and produce energy.

"Such a model would empower local and Indigenous communities to become both producers and consumers of their own energy," said Sergio Villamayor-Tomas, researcher at the Autonomous University of Barcelona and co-author of the study. "This approach can help to democratize the power system and ensure that those most affected by energy projects are also able to benefit from them, which generally has not been the case to date."

While some countries look to hydropower as way to reduce fossil fuel emissions, the study demonstrates that renewable energy does not automatically equal sustainability; rather, it is a unique opportunity to encourage local energy decision-making and reduce dependence on fossil fuels.

"However, with more strategic planning, horizontal governance and community-led solutions, our work demonstrates that the tools for a better path forward already exist," said co-author Maria Claudia Lopez, associate professor at MSU's College of Agriculture and Natural Resources. "The question is not whether governments and investors will meet their energy needs, but [will they] do so without sacrificing the world's vital ecosystems. The tools and frameworks are in place, and this article is one important contribution highlighting them and advancing ongoing discussions of the energy transition."

The research team included:

* Emilio Moran, MSU John A. Hannah Distinguished Professor, Department of Geography, Environment and Spatial Sciences, College of Social Science

* Maria Claudia Lopez, associate professor, Department of Community Sustainability, College of Agriculture and Natural Resources

* Daniel Kramer, professor, James Madison College and professor, Department of Fisheries and Wildlife, College of Agriculture and Natural Resources

* Sergio Villamayor-Tomas, Department of Political Science and Public Law and the Institute of Environmental Science and Technology, Autonomous University of Barcelona

* Researchers at organizations in the U.K., Cambodia, Italy and the U.S. based on a series of workshops sponsored by the National Socio-Environmental Synthesis Center, which is funded by the National Science Foundation.

***

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DOWNERS GROVE, Friday, May 08 2026 - Ahead of this morning's market opening, Dover Corporation has shown some weakness in recent days. Dover Corporation (NYSE:DOV), the NYSE's 5th largest Industrial machinery company by market cap, decreased 0.5% during its previous trading session to close at $219.83.

Dover Corporation (NYSE:DOV) is a diversified global manufacturer with a robust presence in industrial heat exchangers, notably through its SWEP brazed plate heat exchangers, which provide high-efficiency heat transfer solutions for HVAC, refrigeration, and district heating applications. The company leverages innovative compact brazed plate technology to deliver small footprint and energy-efficient heat exchangers, catering to thermal management needs across sectors. Dover's strategic partnerships further enhance its competitive edge in heat transfer solutions. The company's ongoing commitment to technology leadership fosters sustainable growth through custom heat exchanger design and renewable energy applications, solidifying its standing in the market.

Mon Apr 27, 2026: Guild Garage Group Expands with Dover & Company Partnership

Guild Garage Group has announced a significant partnership with Dover & Company, a prominent garage door service provider based in Flint, Michigan. This collaboration marks a pivotal moment for Guild as it expands its footprint into the Michigan market, with the announcement coming on 27th April 2026. Dover becomes Guild's 30th acquisition since the company was launched in 2024, highlighting its rapid growth trajectory in the sector.

Tim O'Reilly, CEO of Guild Garage Group, expressed enthusiasm about the new partnership, stating, "Entering the Michigan market marks an exciting milestone for us as we welcome our 30th partner into the Guild family. This partnership reflects our continued commitment to strategic growth and to aligning with organizations that share our values, culture, and dedication to service." O'Reilly's comments underscore the company's focus on building strong alliances while respecting the core values of its partners, such as those exemplified by Dover.

Dover & Company, founded in 1935, is celebrated for its commitment to quality service and reliability, having developed a strong reputation over nearly a century. Chuck Richards, the owner of Dover, echoed sentiments of mutual respect, stating, "From the very first conversations, I felt that the Guild Team showed genuine respect for the history and core values that have shaped Dover over the years. Their goal wasn't to transform our identity but to reinforce it." This partnership is viewed as a means to leverage Guild's resources while maintaining Dover's long-standing commitment to its customers.

Guild Garage Group continues to seek partnerships with leading garage door service businesses across the nation, emphasizing its strategy to invest in companies with robust management teams and cultures. This approach not only provides financial opportunities for business owners but also enhances service capabilities for customers. Interested parties can reach out to Bennet Crosby at Guild for potential collaboration opportunities.

As Guild Garage Group solidifies its position in the industry with this new acquisition, the company remains focused on creating unmatched growth opportunities for its partners. By allowing owners to retain unit-level ownership, Guild ensures that they continue to benefit from their business's success while expanding their operational capabilities and community impact.

Tue Apr 21, 2026: SWEP Partners with MCD to Broaden Heat Transfer Solutions

DOWNERS GROVE, Ill., SWEP, part of Dover Corporation (NYSE:DOV) and a global leader in brazed plate heat exchangers (BPHEs), announced a strategic partnership with Microchannel Devices (MCD) to expand its offerings in printed circuit heat exchangers (PCHE) on 21st April 2026. This collaboration marks a significant step in enhancing SWEP's market presence and product range, as both companies focus on delivering high-performance heat exchangers that optimize efficiency and support sustainable energy solutions.

The partnership will enable SWEP to access MCD's cutting-edge printed circuit technology, which complements its existing brazed plate technology. According to Ulrika Nordqvist, SWEP President, this collaboration not only enhances SWEP's position in existing markets but also allows MCD to benefit from SWEP's extensive global sales channels and established customer relationships. The synergy between both companies underscores a strong commitment to advancing the green energy transition and addressing market needs in hydrogen infrastructure, energy storage, and CO2 applications.

Through a buy-resell agreement, customers of SWEP will gain access to a diverse portfolio of ultra-compact heat transfer solutions, ensuring seamless procurement processes and established communication channels. The expanded PCHE offerings will be supplied by MCD, an Italian specialist in chemical etching, known for its patented diffusion bonding processes and fully integrated design and production capabilities. This partnership is expected to significantly enhance the customer value proposition by providing solutions tailored to demanding applications characterized by space constraints and extreme operating conditions.

SWEP has been a pioneer in the heat transfer industry since 1983, providing innovative brazed plate heat exchangers that have been integrated into various HVACR and industrial applications worldwide. The collaboration with MCD is poised to further solidify SWEP's role in the global market, as it continues to expand its expertise in sustainable energy solutions. With a workforce of over 1,100 employees and a presence in 50 countries, SWEP is positioned to leverage its capabilities in the Climate & Sustainability Technologies segment of Dover Corporation.

Dover Corporation, headquartered in Downers Grove, Illinois, operates as a diversified global manufacturer with an annual revenue exceeding $8 billion. The company focuses on delivering innovative equipment, components, and solutions across various sectors, including Engineered Products, Clean Energy & Fueling, and Climate & Sustainability Technologies. The strategic partnership between SWEP and MCD is expected to enhance Dover's overall market offerings and its commitment to redefining possibilities in the energy sector.

SECTION 5 CORPORATE PROFILE

5.1 Activities

Dover Corporation, a diversified global manufacturer listed on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in providing innovative industrial products and solutions across various sectors. Its core business activities include engineered components, fluid management systems, refrigeration and food equipment, digital printing technologies, and fueling solutions, serving industries such as manufacturing, energy, and consumer goods. The company operates through five main segments: Engineered Products, Fueling Solutions, Imaging, Pumps and Process Solutions, and Climate and Sustainability Technologies. Among these, the Climate and Sustainability Technologies segment has emerged as one of the fastest-growing, fueled by rising demand for energy-efficient and eco-friendly innovations. Additionally, advancements in digital solutions within Fueling Solutions and Imaging are experiencing accelerated growth, driven by trends in automation and e-commerce. This strategic diversification enables Dover to deliver reliable, high-performance products that support operational efficiency and sustainability for customers worldwide. It is the NYSE's 5th largest Industrial machinery company by market capitalisation.

Dover Corporation (NYSE:DOV) stands as a diversified global industrial powerhouse, excelling in the production of industrial pumps, including centrifugal pumps, positive displacement pumps, and metering pumps. It caters to various sectors with innovative solutions like sanitary food-grade pumps, chemical dosing pumps, and rotary lobe pumps. The firm’s expertise extends to crafting specialized peristaltic hose pumps and slurry solids-handling pumps, crucial for pumps for oil and gas. It provides comprehensive fluid handling systems, complemented by valves and actuators. Additionally, Dover’s portfolio includes heat exchangers and diverse pumps and process equipment. The packaging sector benefits from Dover's printing and coding equipment, featuring continuous inkjet printers, thermal inkjet coders, industrial labeling machines, and case coding and marking systems. In packaging machinery, its offerings include form-fill-seal machines, case packers, and palletizers. Their refrigeration and food equipment are optimal for commercial refrigeration units, cold storage solutions, and food processing equipment. Moreover, they supply fueling dispensers and systems, fuel management systems, industrial automation controls, and advanced sensors and transmitters.
